data.title: Microsoft: The AI Reality Check Investors Can't Ignore
data.description:
The debate over Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is rarely whether to buy, but rather at what price. As I’ve discussed in one of my previous coverages on Microsoft, it’s one of the few companies that’ve consistently delivered double-digit percentage growth, while maintaining sustained margin expansion at scale, despite its sheer scale. This accordingly reflects a durable growth strategy implemented by Microsoft that’s capable of adapting effectively to evolving technological advancements and rapidly changing end-market demand dynamics.

Even during the cyclical downturn in fiscal 2023, when consolidated growth decelerated to single digits, Microsoft’s higher-margin Azure business remained resilient, effectively capturing opportunities from the persistent shift to cloud computing. This has been key to narrowing Microsoft Azure’s gap from key rival and industry leader Amazon Web Services (AMZN), or “AWS,” and reinforcing Azure’s emergence as the leading primary cloud service provider among enterprise multi-cloud strategies today. Azure’s resilience has also been a key enabler of Microsoft's sustained pace of margin expansion, reinforcing free cash flows that underpin its long-term valuation outlook.

With the stock down about 14% since Microsoft reported better-than-expected fiscal Q1 results in late October, the question remains on whether it’s a buy opportunity or value trap still at current levels. This accordingly raises the stakes for its upcoming earnings report, as investors’ focus shifts from whether Microsoft leads in AI to whether its elevated investment cycle is translating into tangible returns.

As I’ve discussed in the previous coverage, Microsoft’s post-Q1 earnings selloff largely reflects investors’ concerns over its uncertain AI returns trajectory. The company reported $35 billion in capex in the fiscal first quarter and warned of further increases through the year to bolster compute supply availability and better address pent-up demand. This directly contradicts its “earlier prediction that [capex] would moderate.” Yet the elevated infrastructure spending trajectory was absent of a corresponding uplift to growth, reflecting compressed ROI expectations that likely led to the stock’s recent correction.

The stock’s weakness was further compounded by subsequent concerns about AI funding risks, particularly at unprofitable startups like OpenAI (OPENAI), which Microsoft has significant exposure to. Meanwhile, key rival Google’s (GOOG/GOOGL) been rapidly catching up on both generative AI innovations and cloud computing market share, with recent momentum for its TPUs and Gemini 3 model taking the spotlight among its Big Three hyperscaler peers.

Looking ahead, Microsoft will need to prove it’s out of the woods with these overhanging challenges and is back on track with expanding additive AI compute returns while maintaining sustained growth and margin expansion across its core operations. In the following analysis, I revisit the minimum expectations that have historically buoyed Microsoft’s moat and expand on the incremental expectations that follow the step-change in capital intensity amid the AI arms race. Although long-term AI-driven TAM expansion remains intact, Microsoft’s operating backdrop remains prone to further near-term challenges before a structural recovery emerges. Taken together, the stock’s risk-reward is likely to remain skewed to the downside until its upcoming earnings can provide validation to a clearer inflection.

Part I: The Givens – Microsoft’s Minimum Expectations

Expectation: Sustained Double-Digit Growth

Microsoft’s massive Microsoft 365 (“M365”) and Windows installed base has long been a key anchor to its valuation. The company has recently disclosed that it’s amassed an installed base of more than 1.4 billion for Windows 10 and 11, with paid M365 seats surpassing 430 million globally. The minimum expectation on this front has been Microsoft’s ability to sustain double-digit revenue growth—a rare sight at this scale.

Specifically, Microsoft has historically demonstrated execution consistency in overcoming the inherent limitations of decelerating installed base expansion, given the sheer size of both products’ market share. While Windows licensing is inherently sensitive to the cyclical nature of PC demand and an increasing shift to “mobile-first” formats, Microsoft’s productivity software portfolio has proven structural resilience.

In addition to maintaining growth through periodic pricing actions, the company’s also been steadily expanding add-on offerings across the core M365 suite and commercial-focused Dynamics 365. This includes enterprise-grade E5 Security, a mission-critical and prioritized add-on for commercial M365 subscriptions. Despite weak enterprise IT budgets during the 2022 to 2023 monetary tightening cycle, E5 demand stayed resilient. The add-on was a key driver of ARPU expansion for Microsoft’s broader Office 365 Commercial business, compounding low-teens paid Office 365 Commercial seat growth through fiscal 2023 to maintain resilience in the company’s Productivity and Business Processes (“PBP”) segment against stiffening macro headwinds at the time.

This accordingly reflects the importance of maintaining resilient upselling opportunities within its installed base—a key minimum expectation for sustaining Microsoft’s double-digit growth profile.

Microsoft’s Execution Outlook

Looking ahead, there are several critical levers to maintaining this baseline expectation. Specifically, the Windows 10 end-of-life refresh, as well as the continued rollout of paid Copilot add-ons, represents key incremental monetization opportunities across Microsoft’s installed base.

Yet the near-term execution backdrop will likely become increasingly challenged.

Specifically, PC shipments face a looming slowdown that threatens to upend previously anticipated tailwinds from the Windows 10 end-of-life refresh cycle and broader AI PC shift. This is largely due to an intensifying memory supply crunch and surging component prices that risk crimping affordability and weakening PC demand. Specifically, the industry expects PC OEMs to raise prices by up to 20% on average this year, with Dell (DELL) planning hikes of as much as 30% on its premium laptop and desktop models to offset the impending memory cost headwind. The anticipated cost pass-through to consumers is expected to decrease PC shipments by up to 8.9% y/y in 2026, with a similar outlook on PC unit TAM compression echoed by OEMs like HP Inc. (HPQ). This could potentially upend Microsoft management’s earlier expectation for “continued momentum from Windows 10 end of support” and risk extending the Windows OEM and Devices business’ exposure to protracted declines.

The near-term headwind on Windows uptake is likely to heighten expectations for Copilot monetization. Specifically, Microsoft’s Copilot Business add-on is currently priced at $21 per user per month, while Copilot for M365 Consumer starts at $9.99 per user per month for Basic and prices up to $19.99 per month per user for Premium. This represents a 10% top-up to the base price for M365 Basic at $99.99 per user per year and Premium at $199.99 per user per year. The impact is even more pronounced on the commercial front, with the Copilot Business add-on almost doubling the total monthly per-seat payment relative to the $22 per-user baseline price for M365 Business Premium.

However, the impending upselling opportunity for Copilot is likely to face intensifying competition. Despite Microsoft’s first-mover advantage on AI-driven productivity tools, thanks to its prescient investment in OpenAI, Copilot has fallen to a distant third place against ChatGPT’s leadership and Google Gemini’s rapid catchup since the rollout of Gemini 3. ChatGPT now commands 810 million monthly active users (“MAUs”), while Gemini has acquired more than 400 million as of mid-2025, rapidly accelerating from just 350 million in March. Microsoft Copilot, on the other hand, has just surpassed 150 million MAUs across use cases in “information work, coding, security, science, health, and consumer.”

The emerging competition is likely to stiffen, as Microsoft’s advantage in proprietary model development remains comparatively lacking. As discussed in my previous coverage on the stock, Copilot’s primary reliance on OpenAI’s GPT LLMs could represent a near-term limitation, and risks limiting durability to Microsoft’s upselling opportunity in the near-term.

Expectation: Sustained Margin Expansion at Scale

Another mainstay pillar of Microsoft’s valuation premium has been its ability to drive consistent margin expansion at scale, thanks to durable operating leverage improvements underpinned by its sustained double-digit growth trajectory. This is especially evident in the PBP segment, given the advantage of its massive M365 installed base and subsequent benefit of inherently lower selling costs for its adjacent add-on software offerings. Additional deployments of both its mainstay productivity software (e.g., M365) and add-ons portfolio (e.g., Copilot) also benefit from near-zero marginal costs, allowing for substantial operating leverage improvements realized as adoption scales.

Microsoft’s Execution Outlook

Yet Microsoft’s margin expansion trajectory remains prone to near-term volatility. Specifically, elevated costs of scaling AI infrastructure investments remain a structural headwind. This is consistent with sustained operating margin compression in the Microsoft Intelligent Cloud segment since the advent of AI investments in early 2023. Although the unit’s margins have just returned to expansion during the fiscal first quarter, it remains far from being accretive at the consolidated level. Looking ahead, Microsoft remains prone to an uneven earnings outlook, as its AI investments across key operating segments (not just Azure) continue to scale, while the related monetization trajectory, as discussed earlier, remains at risk of further challenges.

Part II: Incremental Expectations Driven by the AI Arms Race

Expectation: AI Needs to Deliver Tangible Incremental Returns

What investors now demand is incremental AI returns on top of Microsoft’s existing fundamental durability from productivity software and Windows OEM sales. This is consistent with the stock’s post-Q1 earnings correction, given the step-change in capital intensity related to AI investments lacking evidence of incremental returns.

Specifically, management has repeatedly expressed that end-market demand for Azure AI services continues to exceed compute supply availability. This has resulted in an accelerating RPO backlog that’s encouraged Microsoft to maintain an increasing spending trajectory on GPU and CPU investments, in particular. Yet limited signs of corresponding uplift in its growth and earnings guidance for FY 2026 suggest a potential disconnect, heightening concerns about structural ROI compression on its AI investment strategy.

Microsoft’s Execution Outlook

This accordingly increases urgency for Microsoft to deliver AI ROI visibility at its upcoming earnings update. However, another structural challenge stemming from emerging policy uncertainties appears imminent and risks further thwarting Microsoft’s ability to execute and optimize capitalization of emerging AI opportunities and restore ROI expansion in the near term.

What we already know is that Microsoft, as well as its hyperscaler peers, have been facing extended infrastructure supply constraints. Management has repeatedly disclosed that insufficient infrastructure capacity has remained the primary immediate hurdle to furthering Azure growth and AI share gains:

In Azure, we expect Q2 revenue growth of approximately 37% in constant currency as demand remains significantly ahead of the capacity we have available. And while we're accelerating the amount of capacity we're bringing online, we will continue to balance Azure revenue growth with the growing needs across our first-party apps and AI solutions, our own R&D efforts and the end-of-life server replacements. Therefore, we now expect to be capacity constrained through at least the end of our fiscal year.

Source: Microsoft Q1 Fiscal 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

As discussed in my previous coverage on Microsoft, the bottleneck doesn’t just stop at AI chip supply, but is increasingly expanding into power availability. Specifically, CEO Satya Nadella has recently suggested a growing inventory of chips due to insufficient power supply to bring them online:

…the biggest issue we are now having is not a compute glut, but it’s power — it’s sort of the ability to get the builds done fast enough close to power….So, if you can’t do that, you may actually have a bunch of chips sitting in inventory that I can’t plug in. In fact, that is my problem today. It’s not a supply issue of chips; it’s actually the fact that I don’t have warm shells to plug into.

Source: CEO Satya Nadella, Bg2 Podcast.

However, the situation is now potentially at risk of being further challenged by incoming policy changes under the Trump administration, which is now vowing to hold big tech accountable for surging energy costs stemming from data center operations.

Not only is the ongoing energy supply constraint expected to represent an extended hurdle for demand conversion, but the resulting surge in electricity costs also inherently raises further risks of near-term ROI compression for Microsoft. Specifically, amplifying AI compute demands and new data center builds have already added $6.5 billion in additional U.S. grid costs last year. This has sent Americans’ electricity bills soaring by as much as 267% in 2025 compared to 2020 levels.

The looming cost headwind is further amplified by recent policy uncertainty, given President Trump’s recent proposal to shift the responsibility of data center-driven energy cost inflation and grid expansion expenses onto big tech companies like Microsoft. The impending challenge on Microsoft’s near-term AI returns is corroborated by the company’s subsequent commitment to “pay for the costs of building and using the electric-grid infrastructure required by its data centers.” Specifically, Microsoft has recently introduced the “Community-First AI Infrastructure Initiative” in response to prospects of the impending policy change. The initiative includes five commitments, including a pledge that Microsoft’s data centers will not increase Americans’ electricity prices:

We’ll pay our way to ensure our data centers don’t increase your electricity prices.

…we believe the long-term success of AI infrastructure requires that tech companies pay their own way for the electricity costs they create. This will require that we take four steps, and we’re committed to each:

First, we’ll ask utilities and public commissions to set our rates high enough to cover the electricity costs for our data centers. This includes the costs of adding and using the electricity infrastructure needed for the data centers we build, own, and operate…Second, we’ll collaborate early, closely, and transparently with local utilities to add electricity and the supporting infrastructure to the grid when needed for our data centers…Third, we’ll pursue innovation to make our data centers more efficient…Fourth, we’ll advocate for the state and national public policies needed to support our neighboring communities with affordable, reliable, and sustainable power.

Source: Microsoft Blog.

This could represent an incremental cost headwind, further limiting near-term visibility into Microsoft’s AI returns trajectory and potentially skewing its valuation prospects to the downside.

Fundamental and Valuation Considerations

Taken together, it’s likely Microsoft’s elevated ROI uncertainties will persist in the near-term given continued execution challenges ahead. While I maintain my base case price target of $490 per share for the stock, I believe the looming execution challenges could result in better entry opportunities.

Specifically, based on the foregoing analyzed execution challenges facing Microsoft’s AI monetization and broader operational backdrop, the company remains at risk of near-term growth deceleration—particularly in the More Personal Computing (“MPC”) and PBP segments due to looming PC demand weakness and intensifying competition in AI-driven upselling opportunities. This is further compounded by persistent margin compression headwinds amid rising AI investment costs, alongside risks of added pressure from incremental energy spending needs.

Under the downside scenario forecast, I project limited reacceleration in FY 2026 PBP revenues, as impending Copilot upselling opportunities risk being tempered by intensifying competition and a widening market share gap from rivals ChatGPT and Gemini. The downside scenario forecast also assumes persistent single-digit percentage growth in MPC sales to reflect a slower-than-expected Windows 10 end-of-life refresh cycle due to the memory supply crunch. Meanwhile, Intelligence Cloud reacceleration is also expected to be tempered given ongoing chip and energy supply uncertainties.

These projections yield a downside scenario price of $410 per share. The downside scenario price target is computed under the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) valuation method and considers projections in line with the bear case fundamental forecast above. A terminal value of $3.6 trillion based on the application of a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on Microsoft’s estimated terminal cash flow, as proxied by estimated FY 2030 EBITDA, is considered. The 3.5% perpetual growth rate is in line with the long-term pace of economic expansion across Microsoft’s core markets and reflects the pace at which its steady-state cash flows can sustainably grow at when the business cycle matures. Total cash flow projections, including the terminal value, are discounted at a WACC of 9.6%, which is in line with Microsoft’s capital structure and risk profile.

Final Thoughts

While Microsoft’s prospects for sustaining its double-digit revenue CAGR in the long run, alongside an eventual recovery towards margin expansion at scale, are intact, near-term execution hurdles remain elevated. This accordingly contradicts investors’ heightened expectations for improved AI ROI visibility ahead of Microsoft’s upcoming fiscal Q2 earnings results and risks continued pressure on the stock’s near-term outlook. Taken together, I believe more compelling entry opportunities are likely to emerge in the fiscal second half when clearer evidence of sustained AI monetization and restored operating leverage starts to surface and potentially resets Microsoft’s risk-reward profile more favorably towards the upside.

data.title: Whale's Methodology: Investing Through The Lens Of Volatility (I)
data.description:
In investing, volatility is like the market’s heartbeat: it does not tell investors which way prices will move, but it does tell investors how rough the journey is likely to be. Rather than devoting all our energy to predicting ups and downs, it is often more helpful to understand the shape of risk first—where volatility comes from, when it tends to change, and how to build it into the day-to-day running of a portfolio.

Volatility, in Plain English

Volatility is a measure of how widely an asset’s price moves over a given period. In practice, it is commonly approximated by the standard deviation of returns: the higher the volatility, the more sharply prices swing around their average; the lower the volatility, the more stable the price action tends to be. The keyword here is magnitude, not direction: high volatility does not automatically mean losing money. It simply means outcomes are more dispersed, so both potential gains and potential drawdowns are larger.

From an investment perspective, volatility is typically discussed in two broad categories: historical volatility and implied volatility. The former is what has happened; the latter is what the market is pricing for the future.

  • Historical volatility (HV): calculated by taking the standard deviation of daily/weekly/monthly returns over a past window and annualising it, reflecting how the asset has actually behaved.

Source: IFMC

  • Implied volatility (IV): inferred from option prices and commonly interpreted as the market’s expectation (or pricing) of future volatility. It is closer to a consensus forecast: when sentiment tightens, or tail risks are repriced, IV often moves ahead of realised volatility in the spot market.

Source: IFMC

Crucially, volatility is not a constant. Across different assets, regimes and macro backdrops, it tends to show clustering (periods of high volatility often persist, as do calmer ones), jumps (events can cause sudden spikes), and fat tails (extreme moves occur more often than a normal distribution would imply). Understanding these features helps investors avoid treating volatility as a single, static number.

Source: Deribit

Why Volatility Matters

The real challenge in investing is not whether investors get one call right or wrong; it is whether investors' strategy remains executable through prolonged turbulence. Volatility matters because it is directly tied to what an investor's equity curve can withstand—and, by extension, whether an investor's approach is sustainable. For most investors, the true “risk” is not volatility itself, but the loss of discipline it can trigger: panic selling, levering up into rallies, and constant churn—turning manageable uncertainty into irreversible losses.

At a framework level, volatility matters in at least three ways:

  • It is the language of risk budgeting: if two approaches both deliver 10% annualised returns, but one requires 30% volatility while the other needs only 10%, the latter is far more risk-efficient and better suited to long-term compounding.

  • It acts as a market thermometer: high volatility suggests the market is repricing uncertainty; low volatility may reflect overconfidence or underpriced risk. Each regime calls for different positioning and risk controls.

  • It determines position sizing—and survival odds: if investors do not constrain positions with volatility, their portfolio can inadvertently amplify risk exposure during high-volatility phases, deepen drawdowns, and force investors out at the worst possible time.

Compounding is often the result of avoiding big mistakes. Those big mistakes tend to occur when volatility rises, correlations rise, and liquidity deteriorates. Making volatility part of routine portfolio management is, in effect, preparing for that environment in advance.

The value of volatility is not merely in knowing that an asset is “more exciting”. Its real usefulness is that it can be turned into concrete, repeatable portfolio rules: what to own, how much to allocate, when to de-risk, and when to allow risk back in.

Asset Selection: Start with the Risk Profile

Traditional asset assessment often starts with sector, growth and valuation. A volatility lens adds another layer: a risk profile of price behaviour. For instance, two assets may look similar on fundamentals, yet if their volatilities differ meaningfully, so should position limits, holding horizons and even stop-loss design.

Common screening ideas include:

  • Remove structurally high-volatility assets: assets that remain far more volatile than their peers and recover slowly from drawdowns may be better suited to trading than to long-term allocation.

  • Focus on “low-volatility quality”: lower volatility is often associated with steadier cash flows and more predictable earnings (though it can also reflect temporarily suppressed sentiment, so fundamental checks still matter).

  • Compare implied and historical volatility: when IV sits well above HV, the market is often paying a premium for future uncertainty. That is not automatically bearish; it may point to an approaching risk event or a meaningful fear premium.

The key point is this: volatility is not a standalone buy/sell signal. It is a constraint—it shapes how investors express a view through position sizing and implementation.

Source: IFMC

Portfolio Construction: Volatility & Correlation

Many people treat diversification as simply “owning lots of shares or cryptocurrencies”. Proper diversification comes from lower correlations. Overall portfolio risk is jointly determined by volatility and correlation. Intuitively, when markets come under stress, correlations often rise; positions that appeared diversified can suddenly fall together. This is why a volatility-based approach emphasises that diversification is not a numbers game—it is about whether sources of risk are genuinely independent.

In practice, investors can allocate using a risk budget: aim for each asset to contribute to portfolio risk more evenly, rather than allocating purely by capital weights. A simplified approach is to estimate each asset’s volatility first, then assign lower weights to higher-volatility assets and higher weights to lower-volatility assets, resulting in a steadier portfolio that is easier to hold over the long run.

Ongoing Management: A Volatility Brake and a Rebalancing Trigger

The most direct way to operationalise volatility is to turn it into clear, pre-committed risk rules. Rather than deciding to cut risk only once emotions are running high, investors set the conditions in advance:

  • Volatility rises → reduce risk exposure: if portfolio or core-asset volatility lifts materially, trim high-beta exposure and add defensive assets or cash-like holdings, prioritising investors' ability to stay invested for the next opportunity.

  • Volatility falls → reintroduce risk (gradually): declining volatility suggests stabilising pricing, allowing investors to rebuild risk step by step—while remaining alert to a “low-volatility trap” (extended calm can breed fragility).

  • Use volatility to trigger rebalancing: rebalancing need not be strictly quarterly or annual. If an asset’s volatility regime shifts (for example, persistently moving from low to high volatility), treat that as a change in its risk characteristics and re-weight accordingly.

A core habit worth building is this: any return target must be matched with a volatility constraint. In the face of uncontrollable volatility, return targets can become wishes; volatility constraints are discipline.

The purpose of a volatility lens is to move investing away from “guessing the next move” and back towards managing uncertainty. Once investors can describe assets, assess portfolios, and size positions based on volatility, they are far less reliant on emotion or luck to navigate market cycles. The first rule of investing is to stay alive before trying to scale results—and volatility is the ruler that turns “staying alive” into an executable process.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla Q4 Preview: This May Disappoint, But The Autonomy Talk May Move It Anyway
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) heads into Q4 earnings on January 28th with a familiar setup.

Fundamentals are likely to disappoint, given the 15.6% YOY decline in Q4 EV deliveries and the second straight year of declining volumes, now trailing BYD (BYDDF).

However, TSLA stock has rarely been priced like a car company. Therefore, it is my view that the most important items to monitor in the upcoming earnings release are related to the autonomy story. Among these items, I’d closely monitor any comments related to the January 22 announcement of robotaxi rides without safety monitors in the car and the status of the robotaxi rollout beyond Austin, Arizona, and the Bay Area. Another item that I discuss in this article is the recent push to monetize full self-driving ("FSD"), which could be a tailwind to gross margins this year.

Overall, I believe holding this stock makes sense over a long time horizon of 12-18 months. That said, I wouldn't buy heading into the Q4 print. Below, I discuss why.

The Autonomy Story

In the case of Tesla, fundamentals go last. Q4 deliveries fell 15.6% YOY, below the analyst consensus (418,227 reported vs 434,487 expected). On a full-year basis, deliveries were down 9% year over year. That made 2025 the second consecutive year of declining deliveries.

It is my view that sentiment (most of the time, driven by irrational expectations) is the core driver for this stock. Heading into Q4 earnings, there are quite a few items worth tracking during the earnings call.

First, Tesla said on January 22 that it had started robotaxi rides in Austin without safety monitors in the car.

Or did they?

According to some reports, it seems that some driverless rides are accompanied by a trailing support car. I have little to no doubts that the Q&A session will address this topic. I recommend keeping a close eye on any comments that confirm or reject the existence of trailing support cars, and any timelines for full autonomous rides.

Moving on to the rollout outside of Austin, please take note of CEO Elon Musk's comments last year:

I think we will probably have autonomous ride-hailing in probably half the population of the U.S. by the end of the year.

Let's see what we have outside of Austin. In the San Francisco Bay Area, the company launched a Bay Area ride-hailing service in Q3 last year. Notice the lack of the autonomous word in my statement. Furthermore, California regulators said back in July that the company had not applied for the driverless testing and deployment permits required to operate driverless taxis in the state. This may have changed ever since, so I recommend tracking any comments on this matter in the upcoming earnings release.

In Arizona, the company was approved back in September to test autonomous robotaxis with a safety monitor. In November, they obtained the Transportation Network Company (TNC) permit, which enables them to run a ride-hailing service and charge for rides. However, this permit doesn't allow them to run fully driverless operations.

Back in Q3, Elon said:

I think that's perhaps the most important data point. And then we do expect to be operating Robotaxi in, I think, about 8 to 10 metro areas by the end of the year.

I recommend tracking whether this promise was fulfilled or not in the Q4 call. If it wasn't, I wouldn't short the stock (like any other traditional fundamental-driven, value-based investor). Why?

Any perception of an upcoming deployment could re-rate the stock, even if this deployment is delayed. The November shareholder meeting suggested some interesting cities, including Miami, Las Vegas, Phoenix, Dallas, and Houston, as the next markets.

Another item worth monitoring is the progress on FSD monetization.

Earlier this month, the company confirmed a move away from offering the standard Basic Autopilot functionality from the new Model 3/Y in North America. The idea is to push customers toward a paid FSD subscription. From a pure margin perspective, I like this idea. Why?

Take a look at the evolution of gross margins over the last few years below:

During the Q3 earnings call, management disclosed that paid FSD customers were 12% of the fleet. This is a very interesting KPI that I would definitely monitor in Q4 (likely during the earnings call rather than the results page).

On top of that, we have the global expansion of FSD. This month, Elon said he expects Europe and China to approve FSD soon. Even though the upside from a top-line perspective may be lower than in the U.S. (due to the income per capita in these regions), I would expect a wave of shareholder excitement if a concrete date is set during the earnings call.

Finally, from a product ramp perspective, Elon recently noted that the Cybercab and Optimus initial output will be "agonizingly slow, but eventually end up being insanely fast." From the last earnings call, production for Cybercab was planned for Q2 2026, and for Optimus (1M production line), "hopefully" toward the end of 2026. Knowing how fluid the company is with its deadlines, I would closely monitor any updates to these two timelines during the Q4 call. Also, keep an eye on CapEx. Back in Q3, the company guided $9B for FY25 and noted that they expect a substantial increase in 2026.

Before I go into the next section, I have to say two or three words about the legacy EV car business. I wouldn't bother with the fundamentals of this segment, to be honest. Tesla already lost the battle last year to BYD.

The situation in Europe is raw, to say the least. December data shows a decline in registrations in Sweden of 71% and in France of 66% YOY. On top of that, Q3 saw a tailwind from the end of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit. Q4 is going to be a tough quarter (both top and bottom line) without this tailwind. The Street expects 17% in gross margin and $0.30 in GAAP EPS.

The only bright side in the EV segment that I believe is worth monitoring is the performance of the affordable trims (Model Y/3 trims launched at $39,990 and $36,990, respectively). However, I’d be very cautious if I saw a big uptick in sales. Cannibalization from premium models is a big risk here.

Valuation

What's that? (Some may rightfully say).

The stock is trading at 350x forward earnings and 16x forward sales. Even though that's significantly above the median of the consumer discretionary segment, I wouldn't short the stock based on this (or any fundamental-driven argument). Despite this sky-high valuation, shares jumped by 40% between November and December last year.

Case in point: this is a narrative stock.

Perception is all that matters here, in my modest view.

Conclusion

I think a reasonable rating here is a Hold.

Fundamentals are going to be raw heading into Q4, especially as this quarter will no longer benefit from the end of the EV tax credit tailwind effect. The Street expects an EPS of $0.30, which represents a significant sequential decline from the $0.50 reported in Q3.

That said, Tesla is not trading on the legacy auto business right now. It is my view that the autonomy narrative is the single most important factor driving the stock. Therefore, I would keep a close eye on any signs of progress in expanding robotaxis outside of Austin. Elon suggested in Q3 an expansion toward the 8 to 10 metro areas before the end of the year. Even if this milestone is missed (which I strongly believe it will), any new deployment goals this year could fuel a wave of irrational excitement. I am sure management has a few aces up their sleeves that will reveal in Q4. Some of them could be any developments on the paid FSD strategy and dates for the Europe/China FSD approvals.

I feel optimistic that Tesla management will keep spirits up. That’s why I wouldn’t head for the exit if I had a position, considering a timeframe of 12-18 months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: United Parks & Resorts: Troubles Under The Surface
data.description:
United Parks & Resorts Inc. (PRKS) operates a portfolio of theme parks and other entertainment venues in the United States. The company owns names such as SeaWorld, Busch Gardens, Discovery Cove, and Sesame Place, among other brands. The company’s parks generate very good earnings for PRKS after a Covid-time push for higher monetization.

Concerns are brewing under the surface. A long-term decline in attendance clouds the outlook as consumers are spending less time at theme parks, posing a threat to earnings over the long term. Current macroeconomic weakness isn’t enough to explain weakness in the metric.

PRKS has generated fair, but not very good, long-term returns; the stock has appreciated at a 7.0% CAGR over the past decade. Instead of dividend payments, PRKS has decided to distribute excess capital through a significant amount of share repurchases in the past few years.

PRKS’s Financials Are Great After Covid-Time Monetization Push

PRKS’s current financials are good; core assets continue to generate high earnings and cash flow. With $193 million in CapEx over the past twelve months, primarily including maintenance CapEx and new attractions in existing parks, PRKS’s primary focus is on optimizing current assets’ earnings. Such optimization has been executed well in recent years.

Revenues currently stand at $1.67 billion, showing a slight slowdown from 2024 but standing relatively stable regardless. Better monetization has created structurally higher margins—LTM operating income stands strong at $408 million at a very high 24.4% margin, much better than PRKS’s pre-Covid margin level. The ROIC stands at a good 12.9% level.

Behind the margin increase, alongside an industry-wide push, PRKS managed to improve per-visitor revenue massively during the Covid pandemic in 2021 and 2022 through higher admission pricing and improved in-park spending on food, merchandise, and experience offerings. During the past twelve months, the average visitor has generated $78.6 in revenue for PRKS, up by 27% from $61.8 in 2019. As the theme park business model has a significant portion of fixed costs, a higher amount of per-visitor revenue has trickled down to earnings very well. PRKS has also managed its operating expenses very prudently.

Despite a total of $2.24 billion in interest-bearing debts, PRKS is managing its financial position well and has been able to spend a significant amount of excess capital on share repurchases. There are currently 55.4 million diluted outstanding shares, down significantly from 86.9 million in 2019. On the surface, PRKS’s assets seem highly attractive.

An Attendance Slowdown Weighs on PRKS

There’s a concerning trend behind surface-level financials—a noticeable negative trend in PRKS’s attendance. PRKS’s most recent Q3 report showed a steepening -3.4% decline in the key metric. The disappointing performance was related by management to an unfavorable calendar shift, poor weather, lower international visitation, and poor execution. A lower number of visitors is quick to deteriorate earnings; sharply negative operating leverage led adjusted EBITDA to decline by -16.3% to $216 million for the quarter.

Many of the named factors seem transitory in nature, but I believe that there’s a more fundamental long-term shift underneath—PRKS's attendance has already been on a decline for a long time. Attendance stood at 25.4 million back in 2008 and has since steadily declined to around 21.3 million for the past twelve months. The slowdown has been gradual but consistent, implying a structural trend instead of temporary weather impacts and a consumer slowdown.

PRKS notes lower attendance to represent an opportunity to increase attendance to historical levels, but I believe the trend to be more of a threat. Spending on recreation services continues to grow in the U.S., but there is a shift away from theme parks. Improved Covid-time monetization has made visiting theme parks much more expensive, shifting consumers towards other forms of entertainment. The rise of streaming services seems to play a key part; staying at home and watching Netflix is much cheaper than attending SeaWorld. Six Flags Entertainment (FUN) has shown similarly concerning attendance, as I noted in a September article; the trend is industry-wide.

There’s still clear demand for theme parks, but declining attendance poses a major risk over the long term. PRKS’s margin level has already started to decline from a 30.3% operating margin in 2022; a continuation in the trend could bode very weakly for PRKS’s earnings power. The company’s per-visitor revenue is even on a slight decline in 2025 so far, combining with weak attendance growth. It doesn’t seem likely that PRKS’s much higher post-Covid margins will be sticky if consumers don’t decide to return to theme parks. Price consciousness, especially amid weak consumer sentiment, makes such a return unlikely, in my opinion.

PRKS’s Valuation Is Unattractive as Earnings Are Under Pressure

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for PRKS to estimate a fair value. As a base scenario, I expect weakening attendance to weigh on growth; I only expect 1% perpetual revenue growth from 2026 forward. The figure still assumes an improvement from 2025 as transitory weather headwinds subside.

Lower attendance will inevitably weigh on margins, especially as PRKS doesn’t seem able to increase per-visitor revenue further. I expect the EBIT margin to stabilize at 20% in a base scenario, much lower than currently but still higher than PRKS’s typical pre-Covid margins. PRKS had an average 12.1% EBIT margin during the 2010s.

PRKS should convert earnings well into free cash flow after taxes.

The estimates put PRKS’s fair value at $27.7, suggesting 26% downside as of writing. I believe that while PRKS’s assets seem attractive on the surface and an 11.4 forward P/E doesn’t seem expensive, structural earnings pressure from weaker attendance makes the investment unattractive. For PRKS to return well as an investment, the company needs to stabilize its performance.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6% interest rate, annualized from Q3 interest expenses and debt. I expect quite high leverage from PRKS at a long-term debt-to-equity ratio of 50%.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates PRKS’s beta at 1.28. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.88% and the WACC at 8.09%.

Takeaway

The investment case for PRKS seems weak—while the company’s parks still generate great earnings, a secular decline in attendance poses a long-term threat. Consumers are shifting away from theme parks into other forms of entertainment, especially after the industry’s Covid-time monetization push, likely making the current earnings level unsustainable. I initiate PRKS stock at a Sell rating.

data.title: Tapestry: Fragile Rally Amid Rich Multiples
data.description:
In 2026, I think one of the best moves that investors can make in our portfolios is to emphasize a rotation toward underappreciated rebound sectors, such as retail and apparel. In my view, the elevated valuation multiples amid large-cap tech stocks will drive a meaningful sector rotation this year, and investors should be prepared for this shift.

That said, stock-picking is also key. Amid a broad selloff last year, many individual retail stocks traded at fantastic valuations, despite strong comp sales and fundamental health. And while there's no doubt that Tapestry (TPR), the parent of Coach and Kate Spade, has achieved significant growth in its Coach handbag sales, we have to ask: Can Tapestry fully justify its ~80% rally over the past year?

I'm initiating Tapestry at a "Sell" rating. Though I believe the company is a beacon of strength amid a battered retail sector, defying weakening consumer spending and the headwinds from tariffs, I think Tapestry deserves only a portion of its rally, while the latest move upward has been powered primarily by momentum. In my view, it's a good juncture to lock in gains here.

Accessible Luxury in Handbags and Accessories

Tapestry's core brands, Coach and Kate Spade, are household names that hardly need an introduction. Let's first kick off with a lay of the land on how the company has grown and where its footprint lies.

The chart below showcases some of Tapestry's core metrics. The company does ~$7 billion in annual revenue, emphasizing a DTC (direct-to-consumer model) in which it owns roughly 1,300 stores, as well as its e-commerce channel, which is just shy of a third of overall sales. The company's products touch customers across 30 countries.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's core sales mixes. 56% of Tapestry's revenue comes from handbags, and the second-largest category is accessories (think belts, leather watch straps, sunglasses, and bag charms).

Importantly, also as shown above, 57% of Tapestry's revenue is derived from its company-owned stores, while a much smaller portion of revenue (only 13%) is due to wholesale. This is an important distinguisher for Tapestry. Apparel brands that have relied heavily on wholesale have been disproportionately punished over the last year as department stores and distributors have been wary to take on slower-moving inventory in an uncertain macroeconomic environment (Canada Goose (GOOS), with its declining wholesale revenue despite healthier DTC growth, is a good example of this in the luxury apparel category).

Tapestry estimates the global market for its "premium products" across handbags/small leather goods and footwear to tally up to $105 billion globally. We note that while footwear makes up roughly a third of Tapestry's TAM, its presence in footwear is roughly small at just 6% of overall sales; the company is de-emphasizing footwear, as demonstrated by its August 2025 sale of its Stuart Weitzman to Caleres Holdings, the parent company behind iconic footwear labels like Allen Edmonds, Sam Edelman, and Famous Footwear.

Recent Trends Showcase Strong Growth and Margin Health, but a Major Headwind in Kate Spade

Let's now focus our attention on Tapestry's most recent trends. In mid-November, the company reported fiscal Q1 (September quarter) results, and the key highlights are shown below:

Overall, Tapestry showed strong 13% y/y revenue growth to $1.69 billion in sales, which handily beat Wall Street's expectations of $1.62 billion (+8% y/y). Revenue growth also accelerated five points versus just 8% y/y growth in Q4. We note as well that the quarter was weighed down by a tough compare against Stuart Weitzman in the prior-year Q1, which has since been sold to Caleres: in the absence of that compare, pro forma growth would have been even stronger at 16% y/y.

What I'm less optimistic about, however, is the fact that growth is relatively more one-sided. Coach drove 21% y/y growth, while Kate Spade declined -9% y/y. Yes, it's true that Kate Spade is a smaller piece of the portfolio at just 15% of sales, but in my view, it's the Kate Spade brand that offers Tapestry diversification against Coach's handbag success, and at the moment, Kate Spade's ~$6 million of operating profits (at a ~2.2% margin) is looking at risk to tip into negative territory.

We do note that the company has achieved an improvement in recent revenue declines. The company has reshaped its marketing with the goal of acquiring more Gen Z fans while strengthening its assortment of handbags and achieving a higher mix of full-price sales. At the same time, the company is also reducing the number of overall SKUs in its portfolio, hopefully with the impact of having fewer underperforming labels that it will have to unload later at clearance prices.

Per CEO Joanne Crevoiserat's remarks on the Q1 earnings call on the Kate Spade turnaround:

During the quarter, we made important progress. Our handbag blockbusters outperformed the balance of the offering with higher AUR and strong Gen Z acquisition. This is another example of how our strategic focus is translating into early green shoots in the business.

In Q1, we launched Duo, the hero of our fall campaign, which became the top-performing style in retail, winning with consumers on versatility and value. We also successfully introduced the 454 family in outlet, an on-trend silhouette reimagined from our archives. At the same time, we continued to animate Deco and Kayla, pillars of the assortment that are supporting new Gen Z acquisition.

And as we continue to bring more innovation to the offering, we are streamlining, reducing handbag styles by 40% by holiday, allowing us to stand behind our big ideas with clarity and intention [...]

We know that staying disciplined on discounting will impact our top line results, especially in promotional and highly competitive time periods like holiday, and we are committed to this strategy as we position ourselves for sustainable growth over the long term.

Overall, however, I'm skeptical that Kate Spade can achieve a 180-degree turnaround in Kate Spade; once a brand loses momentum, it's very difficult to reclaim. The core issue here, in my opinion, is that Tapestry is increasingly becoming dependent on its best-selling product: the Coach handbag. Handbags, in theory, have a very long lifespan and no definite replacement cycle, more so than typical apparel. In a down economy, fewer people may choose to treat themselves (or their loved ones) to a new premium handbag, especially if they just bought one in 2025. Tapestry's sale of Stuart Weitzman is essentially an admission that it doesn't think it can be successful in footwear, and the underperformance of Kate Spade also suggests that beyond its core Coach strength, Tapestry doesn't really have a broad, diversified product base to stand on.

Valuation and Q2 Look-ahead

Now we'll come to the lynchpin of my "Sell" rating on Tapestry: while Coach's performance is impressive, I think the stock's sharp rally (nearly doubling over the past year) has overcompensated Tapestry relative to its earnings.

For FY26, the company is guiding to 7%-8% y/y total revenue growth. We are impressed at the company guiding to only ~50bps of margin decay despite a full year of tariff burden (which is expected to be a 230bps headwind, implying a healthy 180bps bump in product margins).

Pro forma EPS is expected at $5.45-$5.60, or 7%-10% y/y earnings growth. At current share prices near $130, this positions Tapestry's valuation at a 23x P/E multiple against this year's guidance. Note as well that a pure P/E standpoint ignores Tapestry's debt load ($2.4 billion of gross debt or $1.6 billion in net debt, stacked on top of a ~$26.6 billion current market cap).

Can we truly justify a premium for Tapestry's stock against the broader S&P 500 when it's only expecting a maximum of 10% earnings growth and is facing struggles in its Kate Spade brand? Tapestry sits at the high end of a broad set of retail comps.

Now, I'd rather bet on iconic brands that are experiencing slower sales (like Lululemon (LULU) and Canada Goose (GOOS)), but offer a cheaper price - rather than pay a premium for Tapestry, which has struggles of its own, especially with its single-product reliance on Coach handbags.

The company is due to report Q2 results, which contain the critical holiday period, in early/mid-February. The core items to watch are:

  • How will Kate Spade's sales trend? We'll need to keep an eye on the impacts of the assortment reduction that the company kicked off in Q1. We're hoping for an improvement in Kate Spade's overall ~2% operating margin profile, but given the possibility for challenging holiday sales, we're not entirely optimistic.
  • Coach deceleration. In a tough macro, I find it very difficult to expect Coach to keep growing >20% y/y against increasingly tough prior-year comps. Handbags are multi-year purchases, and we think eventually slower growth will catch up to Tapestry.

Key Takeaways

In my view, I'd prefer to "Sell" Tapestry on its current strength before earnings. I think the stock will have a tough time justifying its premium ~23x P/E ratio when it's struggling to turn around its secondary brand and faces a tough retail economy. Meanwhile, bargains abound in the sector for longer-term investors willing to have patience and a strong stomach through turnaround stories.

"Sell" this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Netflix: Call It A Dip Or Correction... Either Way I'm Buying (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Netflix (NFLX) has been the center of attention for the past month or so, mainly due to their pending acquisition of Warner Bros Discovery (WBD).

This is partly due to the bidding war between them and nemesis Paramount Skydance Corp. (PSKY), which has been trying its hardest to acquire WBD.

As a result, Netflix has amended the acquisition terms and has made a revised offer to acquire WBD using all cash. This is expected to expedite the acquisition and sweeten the deal for WBD shareholders.

The streaming giant NFLX has also increased its borrowing capacity and looks to secure the deal sooner than expected. I remain bullish on NFLX either way, but I do think the streaming giant will eventually close the deal.

After their recent Q4 earnings, NFLX continued to sell off, reaching a new 52-week low near $80 a share. Whether the sell-off is a valuation concern or due to uncertainty, I believe the stock is oversold and now consider it a strong buy.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Netflix in December, assigning the stock a buy rating and opening a position after the 10-for-1 stock split in November.

Already a high-growth stock, the WBD acquisition was expected to be accretive by the second year and add established franchises from HBO Max.

Despite my buy rating, I warned of share price volatility due to regulatory scrutiny, hostile takeover bids, and increased leverage. As a result, I assumed NFLX would likely halt buybacks, resulting in a potential slowdown in growth.

NFLX also offered double-digit upside to their price target of $134. Since volatility has ensued with the stock down close to 14% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up close to 1%.

Mr. Market Wasn't Impressed

Netflix reported their Q4 earnings to close out the year last week in what I thought was a solid report despite the sell-off. EPS of $0.56 managed to beat analysts' estimates by $0.01, while their top line beat by $80 million.

Revenue amounted to $12.05 billion, representing a strong growth rate of 18% year-over-year.

Despite the double beat, the stock sold off pretty hard, down to near $80. Part of this could also be due to EPS declining from the prior three quarters. Revenue continued its steady climb.

Q4’25

Q3

Q2

Q1

Q4’24

$0.56

$0.59

$0.72

$0.66

$0.43

$12.05B

$11.5B

$11.1B

$10.5B

$10.2B

Most of the recent volatility, as mentioned previously, has to do with uncertainty surrounding the WBD deal approval. Despite this, I'm quite confident Netflix will receive approval in the near future.

NFLX's 18% growth was driven by solid regional performances where the U.S. & Canada saw growth of 18.1%. EMEA showed strong growth too, up 17.6%. Both amounted to $5.34 billion and $3.87 billion, respectively.

Advertising revenue also performed well, reaching 2.5x to $1.5 billion. And management stated for 2026 that this will be a focal point going forward, expected to reach $3 billion.

With Netflix focusing on live events, podcasts, & sports, I think this is a secret weapon that will allow the company to pay down the debt from the pending acquisition. So, for those worried about the balance sheet, this is minuscule in my view.

Operating margins were up three basis points to 29.5%. However, these declined from the previous quarters, likely another reason for the knee-jerk reaction. In the prior quarter, margins were 28.2% and above 30% in Q2 and Q1.

So, a bit of margin retraction. But for 2026, management expects these to rise above 30% in the upcoming quarter and has a target of 31.5% for the year. And focusing on growing ad revenue will likely contribute to margin expansion. You can see from the chart below these are expected to continue trending upward.

Double-Digit Forecast for 2026

During earnings, management also released their Q1 forecast for the fiscal year, which I thought was solid. Revenue is projected to see double-digit growth of 12% to 14% for the year

This is expected to be in a range of $50.7 billion to $51.7 billion, a growth rate of 13.27% at midpoint. This is slightly below the 16% growth in 2025 to $45.2 billion. For Q1, revenue is expected to be $12.157 billion.

So, growth is expected to slow a bit. As mentioned in my prior article, I also expect Netflix to halt share repurchases, at least for the next year or two. This could also impact growth.

But for a company of NFLX's size, I still believe they can see 10% - 15% growth going forward. And this should allow for share price expansion over the long term.

Growth in Key Metrics

Besides increases in net and ad revenue, Netflix experienced solid growth in other areas as well. Subscribers reached 325 million, up from approximately 300 million the prior quarter.

Operating income reached $2.957 billion, up from $2.273 billion the year prior. For the first quarter, this is projected to be $3.9 billion. Net income also grew solidly from $1.869 billion to $2.419 billion year-over-year.

Projections put this at $3.264 billion in Q1. Although Netflix doesn't pay dividends, cash from operations & free cash flow grew as well despite the decline from the prior quarter.

This is essential for a high-growth business, as growing cash flows allow the company to reinvest back into the business and pay down debt for future growth. These amounted to $2.112 billion and $1.872 billion, respectively.

In Q3, cash from operations was $2.825 billion, while free cash flow was $2.660 billion. But both saw growth from the previous year's $1.537 billion and $1.378 billion.

So, to put this into perspective, NFLX grew net (record) and ad revenue, increased view hours 2%, grew subscribers 8%, expanded margins slightly, increased net income 29%, and managed to grow cash flows year-over-year.

While they're likely to remain volatile pending the WBD acquisition, Netflix's performance shows the business continues to remain healthy and growing.

Moreover, the potential acquisition of HBO Max is a catalyst, as this will allow the streaming giant to push out more content, series, and films. And this would likely drive subscriber growth.

Long-Term Upside

With the brutal sell-off, Netflix now appears fairly valued here. Although some may still say it's not cheap enough, their high, double-digit growth and healthy business warrant a premium.

Moreover, I think we may be near a bottom, unless the deal falls through. If so, I see the stock going into the low $70s, maybe even high $60s.

But, if you're a long-term investor, then Netflix offers compelling upside if you're willing to hold for a few years. In the short to medium term, further volatility is likely.

And although growth is expected to slow a bit, the acquisition likely getting approved and their upside potential to their price target of $131 keep me bullish.

Risks & Conclusion

As a result of the pending acquisition, Netflix will likely see high amounts of volatility. While I'm confident that the deal gets approved, this is not certain. Currently, I say about 60/40.

Furthermore, failing to acquire Warner Bros could result in a loss of subscribers due to increased competition from competitors like YouTube.

YouTube is now hosting films, sports, and big events like the Oscars. As a result, this also increases competition in ad revenue.

And lastly, investors should keep a close eye on margins in the coming quarters. Although I expect NFLX to see steady margin expansion due to higher ad revenue, failing to do so may likely result in further selloffs.

However, Netflix is likely to see sustainable, double-digit growth going forward. Furthermore, after the steep sell-off and my confidence in them successfully acquiring Warner Bros., I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy.

data.title: Commodities Lead Major Asset Classes So Far In 2026
data.description:
Prices for raw materials have rocketed higher so far this year, outperforming the rest of the major asset classes by a wide margin through Friday’s close (Jan. 23), based on a set of ETFs. Foreign stocks are currently strong, but they remain distant second-place winners.

The WisdomTree Commodity Index Fund (GCC) has surged 10.7% year-to-date. GCC maintains a relatively diversified portfolio, and so it’s not overly dependent on oil and energy, in contrast with some of its competitors. As a result, GCC’s bull run of late highlights that commodities overall are posting strong gains.

In second place this year: foreign equities, led by stocks in emerging markets (VWO) via a 5.6% return. In close pursuit: shares in developed markets ex-US (VEA), which are up 5.3%.

All the major asset classes are posting gains this year, albeit delivering a wide spectrum of results. A broad measure of the US investment-grade bond market, including Treasuries, is the weakest performer in 2026, rising a thin 0.2%, based on the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND).

US stocks are a middling performer year to date, rising 1.6% via Vanguard Total US Stock Market ETF (VTI).

Within the commodities space, precious metals are especially hot. The SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) have soared 15.6% in 2026 through Friday’s close. Early trading today (Jan. 26) points to a further rally this week as the price of the metal trades well above $5,000 an ounce for the first time. Silver (SLV) is even hotter, skyrocketing more than 44% so far in 2026.

“The rise in precious metals prices is breathtaking and profoundly scary,” wrote Robin Brooks, a senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, on Sunday. The rally is “part of something much bigger,” he advised. “We’re at the start of a global debt crisis, with markets increasingly fearful governments will attempt to inflate away out-of-control debt.”

CNBC, citing a Goldman Sachs report, notes: the demand base for gold has broadened and Western ETF holdings have climbed by about 500 tons since the start of 2025.

A key factor in gold’s rally: a weak US dollar. The two assets tend to move inversely with one another. The US Dollar Index fell 1.2% this year through Friday’s close and has declined nearly 10% over the past year.

Morningstar.com warns against chasing gold after such a strong run, but some analysts still expect higher prices in the near term.

“I think [gold] could well have some further upside,” said Steve Miller, an investment strategy adviser at GSFM, an Australia-based asset manager. “But just as good is it might insulate you from turmoil in other asset classes.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Capital One Financial: A Strong Buy After The Selloff
data.description:
A few months ago, I wrote an article about Capital One Financial Corporation (COF), one of the largest banks in the United States and a premier issuer of branded credit cards.

With tremendous exposure to one of my favorite asset classes—consumer - consumer credit - I argued that Capital One was well-positioned to grow earnings per share at a faster-than-average rate, especially considering the company’s Discover Financial acquisition, which promised to unlock significant synergies.

Fast forward to the present day, and my rating has proven a bit of a mixed bag.

While shares of COF are positive, they have underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) more broadly over the last eight months. They were strongly outperforming until some recent bumps brought shares back in line:

In the last few weeks, Capital One has been in the news due to the firm’s recent earnings report, Brex acquisition, and recent government comments about capping credit card interest rates.

In the last few trading sessions, shares are down roughly 15% as a result of these catalysts.

That said, I believe the recent selloff is a significant opportunity to continue building a position in Capital One, not a sign of structural issues with the company.

Poised to grow earnings per share in the mid-teens range over the next three years - while growing the company’s business banking capabilities significantly - I view shares as a Strong Buy.

Today, I’ll highlight President Trump’s recent comments about credit cards, outline the company’s recent earnings, and dive into the recent Brex acquisition to make the case that shares of Capital One remain an attractive place to invest your capital.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start by taking a look at the company’s recent earnings report. At the end of last week, Capital One reported Q4 earnings, with earnings per share of $3.86 along with revenue of $15.58 billion:

These quarterly revenues mark an all-time high and considerable year-over-year growth of more than 50%. They also topped analyst estimates by more than $100 million.

To be clear, most of this top-line growth is from the DFS acquisition; the company's organic top-line growth is closer to the mid-single-digit range.

On the earnings side, the company missed analyst estimates and actually guided down, which is the primary reason the stock went tumbling nearly 8% on Friday:

The reason for the earnings miss was, according to management, increased investments the company has been making in things like technology, AI, and the premium credit card space.

These investments should, in management’s words, put some upward pressure on the company’s efficiency ratio in the near term:

We've been investing in many of our opportunities for years, like building our heavy spender franchise with consumers and small businesses at the top of the credit card marketplace, building a national franchise of primary banking relationships in our retail banking business and building a modern technology and data infrastructure. And as we continue to build on foundational tech investments to migrate up the tech stack, we're generating new growth opportunities like Capital One Travel, Capital One Shopping and Auto Navigator. Our tech stack was built from the outset working backwards from the AI revolution, and we are now building AI solutions across our businesses...

In recent quarters, we've talked about these opportunities, and we have indicated that collectively, these investments will put upward pressure on efficiency ratio in the near term, and we continue to lean into those.

However, despite the short-term EPS impact, I view these investments as largely strategic given the increase in competition in the premium credit card space and ongoing, long-term structural cost savings that could be achieved through technology and AI optimization.

As such, I see the earnings guide-down as more of a blip than any long-term issue.

Additionally, the company affirmed that long-term synergies with Discover would likely still be intact and represent similar numbers to what Capital One anticipated pre-merger.

This supports the idea that Capital One remains one of the premier consumer credit assets on the market today.

Additionally, beyond the report, a number of other catalysts also had an impact on Capital One's stock in the last few weeks.

Recently, President Trump said that he would call on Congress to cap credit card interest rates, highlighting the strong profitability of credit card companies and the growing financial stress felt by millions of American households.

While the idea makes sense in theory, consumer credit lenders typically price loans according to a customer’s ability to make payments on those loans. The higher the risk, the higher the rate needs to be.

Essentially, a limit would act as a price cap on consumer credit, and in the event that it goes through, there would likely be a lack of consumer credit supply for risky borrowers. I do not see a world where lenders price high-risk consumer credit at a 10% annual APR. Purely on the face of it, I do not expect a motion like this to make any headway in Congress, even if the potential price cap only lasts for a year.

Not only that, but putting a cap on rates would also drive high-risk borrowers to less regulated products like buy-now-pay-later and P2P loans, which in some ways can be even more predatory.

Make no mistake, if a one-year moratorium or price cap goes into effect, it would have a significant impact on Capital One. That said, I do not believe such a cap will be instituted, and thus the recent selloff has likely priced this news adequately - at least in my view.

Finally, on the bright side, Capital One recently purchased Brex, a financial technology company specializing in business finance services:

Right now, Capital One offers consumer credit cards, checking and savings products, and auto loans. It also offers business checking, savings, and credit cards, along with a relatively small capital markets business for institutional clients.

In my opinion, the Brex acquisition is incredibly smart. Brex has a fully integrated business financing stack that includes credit cards and bank accounts, but also vendor payments, travel expenses, and automated accounting tools.

Whether Capital One keeps Brex as a standalone branded product or integrates the firm's software into its native business offering, I expect the company will see considerable new customer acquisition due to the strength of the offering. I do not expect Brex’s tools or infrastructure to provide meaningful cost synergies for Capital One, but rather, ongoing growth and new/sticky customer acquisition. It's also a mostly complementary offering - with a vertical tech platform and COF's existing equity, Brex's margins and offering become even more competitive.

I expect the Brex acquisition will be accretive for COF shareholders, especially over the long term.

At $5 billion - or roughly two years of TTM net income - this is not a small investment by the company, but I believe it's a bet that will ultimately pay off, and then some.

Valuation

On the valuation side of things, shares of Capital One currently trade at roughly 11x blended earnings.

Since 2020, shares have traded between roughly 4.2x and 17.9x earnings, which makes the current P/E set in the middle of the range:

Thus, on a pure valuation basis, investors can expect in-line appreciation with the company’s organic growth. That is, I don't expect there to be a significant long-term impact from valuation, one way or the other.

However, given that earnings growth is expected to come in at roughly 4%, 23%, and 14% YoY over the next three years respectively, I believe there is a significant investing opportunity here where shares could increase by 50% over the next 36 months, assuming no multiple expansion:

If growth comes in even higher than that, potentially as a result of the Brex acquisition, I would expect an additional uplift from the multiple, which could drive even stronger returns.

Positioned in the historically stable consumer credit business with significant opportunity for growth ahead and shares priced at a long-term midpoint, I view shares of COF as a Strong Buy in the present market.

Finally, on a more nominal note, Capital One remains priced at a discount versus the market at roughly 25x earnings, and other banks like SoFi (SOFI), American Express (AXP), and Synchrony Financial (SYF), whose businesses trade between 8x and 70x earnings:

Risks

Of course, there are some risks associated with investing in Capital One, some of which I have already discussed. A potential one-year 10% interest rate cap on credit cards - if passed into law - would obviously damage the company’s net interest margins, and a recent slowdown in EPS expectations has also put a damper on shares.

I view those risks as being substantially priced in already.

In a more macro sense, COF is still considerably exposed to consumer credit, and while earnings have been stable thus far, continued deterioration in consumer sentiment under the Trump administration could lead to materially lower-for-longer earnings for the company, which would obviously have an impact on the stock price:

Finally, shares appear priced at a relative earnings midpoint between mid-single-digit and mid-teens multiples. I view this as entirely neutral for the stock price, as it does not add to or subtract from my organic growth view.

However, if fears about the above risks become sharper in the coming months, Capital One may see its blended price-to-earnings ratio fall back into the single digit range, negating potential stock price gains that could otherwise come from organic growth.

Summary

Overall, there are some risks associated with consumer credit, to which Capital One is heavily exposed. Recent comments from the administration and ongoing investments in technology and AI all present potential earnings headwinds.

However, COF's strong consumer credit franchise, solid growth trajectory, reasonable entry price point, and exciting (and complementary) Brex acquisition make the stock a Strong Buy in my view.

Cheers~

data.title: S&P 500 Falls And Recovers As Geopolitical Pendulum Swings
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) experienced some geopolitical turmoil during the holiday-shortened trading week ending on Friday, 23 January 2026. The index plunged two percent on Tuesday, 20 January 2026, thanks to a one-two punch.

One of the punches was delivered by President Trump's weekend announcement he would impose new tariffs on European nations opposing his initiative to acquire Greenland from Denmark. That action had sent European stock prices lower before contributing to the U.S. stock market's drop. The good news here, however, is that by Thursday, the dispute was resolved and stock prices recovered.

The second punch was delivered by a developing crisis for Japan's economy. On Tuesday, 20 January 2026, the nation's new prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, announced plans for a snap election to secure a mandate for much higher government spending. That's a problem because Japan's central bank has been struggling to control inflation, which the new spending is expected to aggravate. The response by markets has been to send Japanese government bonds and its currency plunging, which has a global impact. This developing crisis has not yet been resolved.

In reaction to all this and other market-moving news, the U.S. stock market rebounded, but not all the way. The S&P 500 closed out the week at 6,915.61, down 0.35% from its previous week's close.

It's not often we can point to geopolitical events as contributing anything more than noise to the trajectory of stock prices. The latest update of the alternative futures chart shows the S&P 500's trajectory is where it would be expected to be for investors focusing on the upcoming future quarter of 2026-Q2.

As for why, one major reason is provided by the CME Group's FedWatch Tool, which continues to project the Fed will hold the Federal Funds Rate steady until 17 June (2026-Q2), when it anticipates a 77% probability for a quarter-point rate cut. The tool forecasts a better than 50% chance of another quarter-point reduction on 28 October (2026-Q4).

As for what else influenced investor expectations for the future during the week that was, here are the week's market-moving headlines:

Tuesday, 20 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil rises on Kazakh supply disruptions, upbeat economic data; Greenland in focus
    • Trump's Greenland threat puts Europe Inc back in tariff crosshairs
      • Trump says he will '100%' follow through on tariff threat - as it happened
      • Trump promises ‘good’ Greenland plan; Europe pushes back - as it happened
  • Fed officials job security to be determined by U.S. Supreme Court:
    • Supreme Court tests limits of Trump's power over the economy
      • Cook case could lead to 'cause' protections for Fed, or a roadmap for dismissals
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • China must strike right balance between demand and supply, says President Xi
      • China hits 2025 GDP growth target on export boom, but can't shake domestic chill
        • China can't make consumers buy goods, so it leans on services to drive economy
        • As US orders fade, Chinese salespeople face tough grind in new markets
        • China’s Trillion-Dollar Illusion
  • BOJ officials signal more hikes to Japan's interest rates ahead:
    • BOJ to signal more rate hikes as yen, politics fuel inflation risks
      • Japan PM Takaichi to call Feb 8 snap election on spending, tax cuts and defence
      • Buyers flee Japanese debt as Takaichi hits the ground spending
    • Japan’s long-bond yields surge to record highs. Why it may be a problem beyond Tokyo
      • Instant View: Japan bond yields soar as election promises stir fiscal fears
  • ECB officials to get new Number Two official, say new tariffs no big deal for Eurozone:
    • Croatia's Vujcic nominated as ECB's next vice president
    • Fresh tariffs to have muted inflation impact in Europe, ECB's Villeroy says
      • European stocks drop the most in two months on Trump tariff threat over Greenland
  • Nasdaq dropped over 2% as Wall Street closed deep in the red on geopolitical jitters
    • Stock market today: Dow plummets 800 points, S&P 500, Nasdaq sink over 2% as Trump's Greenland threats clobber stocks
      • Gold notches record above $4,700/oz, silver hits all-time high

Wednesday, 21 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Trump drops European tariff threat and announces framework Greenland deal - as it happened
    • Trump signs order to limit Wall Street investors in single-family housing
    • WTI oil prices fall as risks from Kazakh production halt subside
    • US construction spending rebounds in October amid renovations
    • US pending home sales plunge to five-month low in December
  • Fed officials expected to hold rates steady until it gets new management:
    • Fed to hold rates through March, and possibly through Powell's tenure - Reuters poll
    • Trump says he may be down to one candidate to head the Fed
  • Bigger trouble developing in Japan, calls for BOJ and JapanGov officials to stabilize currency:
    • Exclusive: Japan should respond 'decisively' to bond selloff, opposition head says
    • Japan's top labour union group urges government to stabilise forex
  • ECB officials getting excited about digital currency:
    • ECB's Panetta says banks will need to issue fully digital private money
  • Wall Street rallied higher after Trump spoke at Davos and eased tariff concerns
    • Wall Street ends higher as investors cheer Greenland framework deal, averted tariffs

Thursday, 22 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • New trade map takes shape in Davos as world adjusts to Trump tariffs
    • Silver rally strains global supply as retail demand surges across Asia
    • US consumer spending rises, labor market remains in lackluster shape
    • Trump's mortgage-backed bond purchases not moving needle on housing costs
    • US third-quarter economic growth revised slightly higher
  • Fed officials' future job security lies in Supreme Court decision, still not seeing getting inflation they predicted from tariffs:
    • US Supreme Court sees risk in Trump running roughshod over Fed
    • Fed's Favorite Inflation Indicator Refuses To Show Any Signs Of Runaway 'Trump Tariff' Costs
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China's rural banks struggle to sell seized properties despite hefty discounts
  • Bigger exports developing in Japan, worry about U.S. trade policy; BOJ officials going through their options to deal with bond rout:
    • Japan's exports up for fourth straight month, US dip clouds outlook
    • Japan retains cautious economic view, flags risks from US trade policies
    • What are Japan's policy options to soothe its bond rout?
      • Small but rising opposition party warns BOJ raising rates too rapidly
  • ECB officials think they've got everything covered with their monetary policy for the Eurozone:
